c74object.name: tap.808.kick~
c74object.digest: TR-808-style bass drum voice, synthesized from the analog circuit
c74object.description:
The tap.808.kick~ object is a circuit-informed model of the Roland TR-808 bass drum voice, built block for block from the original schematic (after Werner, Abel and Smith's DAFx-14 analysis): the pulse shaper, the bridged-T resonator with its feedback regeneration loop, the ~6 ms attack frequency jump that gives the drum its punch, the retriggering pulse, and the leakage nonlinearity behind the characteristic downward pitch "sigh" — all emergent from the modeled circuit rather than bolted on. Trigger it with a signal: a rising edge fires the voice, and the amplitude of the edge (0..1) is the accent. Accent is the trigger bus voltage (4-14 V on the hardware), so an accented hit excites the circuit harder — more punch, slightly different pitch trajectory — not merely louder. Filter states persist across triggers, so fast rolls interfere with the ringing tail like the hardware (no "machine gun effect"). The decay, tone, and level attributes mirror the hardware panel knobs; tuning, pulse, sigh, and attack are circuit bends beyond the panel (component substitutions on the modeled schematic), stock at their defaults. Single-channel: wrap in mc. for stacked or parallel voices.
The eight tap.808 voices share one accent-bus contract; the family overview patcher (tap.808.maxhelp) demonstrates them together on a running pattern.

inlet.digest: Trigger: a rising edge fires the voice; edge amplitude (0..1) is the accent

method.name: signal
method.description: A rising edge on the inlet triggers the drum, sample-accurately. The value the signal rises to (0..1) is the accent: it sets the trigger pulse voltage (4-14 V on the hardware bus), driving the circuit harder rather than scaling the output. A click~, the output of a sequencer row, or any impulse/gate works.

method.name: bang
method.description: Trigger the drum at full accent (from the scheduler; use a signal trigger for sample accuracy).

method.name: trigger
method.description: Trigger the drum with an accent value (0..1, default 1).

method.name: clear
method.description: Reset all circuit state, silencing any ringing tail.

attribute.name: decay
attribute.description: Note length, 0..1 (default 0.5). The hardware decay pot in the bridged-T's regeneration loop: near 0 a tight thump, near 1 a note that rings for seconds — the classic long 808 boom.

attribute.name: tone
attribute.description: Attack brightness, 0..1 (default 0.5): the passive low-pass after the resonator. 1 keeps the click (~7 kHz corner) so the attack cuts through a mix; 0 rounds it off (~300 Hz).

attribute.name: tuning
attribute.digest: Circuit bend: pitch ratio (default 1 = stock ~49 Hz)
attribute.description: Pitch as a ratio of the stock tuning, 0.25..4 (default 1). Scales the bridged-T arm capacitors — the component-substitution mod that makes the drum tunable. The stock schematic rings at about 49 Hz.

attribute.name: pulse
attribute.digest: Circuit bend: trigger pulse width in ms (default 1 = stock)
attribute.description: Trigger pulse width in milliseconds, 0.05..50 (default 1, the hardware CPU's pulse). Longer pulses push the excitation edges apart and change the attack.

attribute.name: sigh
attribute.description: Depth of the characteristic downward pitch relaxation, 0..1 (default 1). 0 is the "disconnect the sigh" hardware mod: the note holds its pitch as it decays.

attribute.name: attack
attribute.description: Depth of the ~6 ms attack frequency jump, 0..1 (default 1). This shift is where the drum's punch comes from — at 0 the same trigger barely couples into the resonator and the note collapses to a soft, quiet thud.
